text: Thank you all for attending this talk. It's kind of hard to put all the work that I've been involved with into a single box. But the title I've landed on is the physical quantification of the interactions between environment, physiology, and human performance. And if that doesn't make any sense, hopefully it make a little bit more sense by the end of the talk. So the starting place of all this is the fact that our environment has a tremendous impact on our bodies and performance. An example of this is the role air quality makes on our health. So there are very well-known health effects uh negative health effects of poor air quality, short-term things such as allergies. It's spring, uh allergy season, so maybe some people are experiencing this right now, coughing, headache, exacerbation of asthma, but also long-term disease such as lung cancer, heart attack, stroke, and dementia, Alzheimer's. According to the World Health Organization, in 2015, over 9 million about 9 million people died worldwide due to bad air quality. But there's more to the story here because all of us have this thing in our heads called a brain. And poor air has also been associated with cognitive decline, reductions in cognitive performance. So maybe you've seen headlines such as, "Is air pollution making you dumb?" The more scientific version is the role of air pollution and cognitive impairment and decline. But still, air quality with the significant number of impacts it has on our physiology is just one aspect of the environment. There's temperature which can cause heat and cold. Stress has a significant impact on our sleep cycles, on our cognitive health and performance, on our ability to thermorreulate and maintain homeostasis. There's light which allows us to see the world around us. Also plays a tremendous role in our sleep cycles, affects our pupil size, our mood, alertness. There's chemo sensation, which we experience through smell and taste, but also if some chemical gets on your skin or into your eyes, it irritates you. And then there's also the constant sensing of the partial pressure of oxygen and carbon dioxide in our blood which is key for all the cells in our body. There's sound which can grab our attention. There's the emotional context. So not just the physical things that we interact with but how we interpret and understand these things and so much more. The goal of this research is to capture this full picture to enable holistic analysis. A lot of work has been done in pursuit of this goal. And then this is just like a brief timeline of some key points of my graduate school education. Some program landmarks are highlighted in red. Nine different publications where I'm either a first author or co-author are shown in green here. Two different awards are shown in black awards and honors. And then for the past six semesters, we've been mentoring different groups of computer science undergraduates for their senior design projects. And those are shown in blue here. So the way I'm going to outline this talk is I'll start with the motivation why we want to do this work and then talk about two key ingredients for making this research possible which are one holistic sensing. The goal of holistic sensing is to capture a comprehensive view of a system of interest. And then the second key ingredient is machine learning which allows us to take this large volume of data that we collect and make sense of it. And then I'll zoom into three specific projects that are all connected to this goal. So the motivation of this work again, we know the environment affects us, but the full story of how the environment impacts our body, our physiology, our performance is far from understood. And part of the problem is the interactions that we're interested in here are very highdimensional. There's a lot of them and they're interconnected. So we pursue a solution to this problem through two key ingredients. The first being holistic sensing which again is the pursuit of a comprehensive snapshot of a system of interest and machine learning which allows us to make sense of this snapshot. This idea of holistic sensing sits at the cornerstone of what we do in our research group. So our research group is called mints multiscale integrated interactive intelligent sensing and simulation. All the work that I'm going to be talking about today fits into this larger vision of mints. This holistic analysis of the environment is enabled through these eight different sensing sentinels which is all the way from global satellite data down to the hyper local of robotic boat, a ground survey vehicle, walking robot and then a 24/7 distributed sensor networks. But more recently, we've expanded into the human domain, the biometric domain. And bringing these two key pieces together has been my focus for the past few years. And the second key ingredient is machine learning. And this basically allows computers to learn by example. So if you want to do machine learning, there are two key requirements. First is a comprehensive data set which we can achieve through this holistic sensing paradigm. But also you need a machine learning algorithm. Typically what this looks like is you'll have two different types of variables in your data set. You'll have one target variables which are quantities that you're trying to estimate or predict. And then you also have predictor variables which are the quantities you use to make that estimation or prediction. What the machine learning algorithm does is it learns the connection between the predictor variables and the target variables. Machine learning can be broken down to two general steps. The first step is training. So here you'll take part of your comprehensive data set to form a training data set. You'll feed this data to machine learning model and out of the model comes a machine learning model. Essentially a computational representation of the relationship between your predictor variables and your target variables. The second step is prediction. So, you'll take new data that your model has never seen before. You'll give it to your model and it'll spit out an estimation for that new data. I'll be focusing on just three studies here, even though there's more to talk about, but all three of these studies approach this overarching goal of quantifying the interactions between environment, physiology, and human performance from three different angles. And they have a shared experimental paradigm which make use of these two key ingredients I was talking about earlier, namely holistic sensing and machine learning. So the first study, this was the first thing I started working on when I started working with Dr. Larry. The title of this work is modeling autonomic pupilary responses from external stimuli using machine learning. So basically the goal here is to model the relationship between pupil size and ambient light information. The first key concept here is light. The light that we commonly interact with is typically not made up of a single color. So, if anyone is a fan of Pink Floyd or has seen this illustration before, you've seen white light entering a prism and then a rainbow coming out. And what this is depicting is that white light is made up of several constituent frequencies. It's made up of several constituent colors. You can characterize this property of light through its spectrum. There's some different examples of light spectra here. The thing to look at is the x- axis are showing wavelength, which is essentially the color of the light. And then the y-axis is showing the intensity. There's some examples like daylight and sunset. Even though they're coming from the same thing, the sun, since the solar angle is changing throughout the day, what we see here at the surface of Earth changes dramatically over the course of a day. But then you also have artificial light sources such as fluorescent bulbs, incandescent bulbs, and LED bulbs which have very different light spectrum. So you can use this frequency information to characterize different light sources. Then this second key part of this study is how we perceive light. We decode light information. We see light through our eyes. And basically how this works is light enters the eye. It gets focused onto the retina which is essentially a stack of cells. And a subset of these cells are photosensitive. So basically they respond and change when presented with light. There are two general types of well there's there's more but two I'll talk about here photosensitive cells. The first are rods which are involved in these low light vision or skotopic vision and then there also cones which allow us to see color. They capture frequency information. And then of these cones there are actually three different types. There's a type of cone that's more sensitive to blue light so-called shortwave photo receptors. There's another more sensitive to green light and then another class more sensitive to greenish yellow light. That's what is being shown here. This is just the response curves, the sensitivity curves of these three classes of photo receptors which we'll come back to later on in the talk. Another key part of this story are the pupils which are the dark part of the eye. This is what controls the amount of light that is shown onto the retina. The pupils actually change size and when they increase in size, it's called pupil dilation. When they decrease in size, it's called pupil constriction. And this is largely an autonomic response, meaning it happens automatically. Most people can't control the size of their pupil on demand. It's typically something that can be indirectly affected through different means. There are a few different things that can modulate pupil size. The first is light. As you go into a dark room, if you could look at your pupils, they would actually increase in size. And if you were to walk outside, your pupils would actually shut down and get as small as possible, presumably to protect your retina. You don't want to burn out your retinas. The second thing that can modulate pupil size is actually cognitive load. Exerting mental effort, so to speak. But this is like trying to solve the math problem. What's 81 * 17? If someone were to look at your pupil while you're thinking and try to figure that out, they would actually see your pupils increase in size. Another thing that modulates pupil size is viewing distance. When you switch from looking at something far away to something that's close to you, your pupils will tend to constrict. In this study, we're directly measuring light and then in some sense, we're indirectly measuring these other things, which we'll get into later. So the way we're capturing this information, one information about our environment or the ambient light and then information about the pupils is through these two sensing systems. In this work, data is collected during three outdoor walks for a single participant which was actually me. And then this is a machine learning pipeline. So we have our input variables or predictor variables. This gets fed into a machine learning model that we develop and train here. This actually uses an ensemble of decision trees for regression. I won't get into all the details of that, but I'll just say that for the afficionados out there. And then the outputs are actually three different targets which generate three separate models and those are the left pupil diameter, right pupil diameter and then the average of the two. The way to look at this is this top colorful plot is showing the light spectra over time. The y-axis is showing wavelength or essentially the color of the light. And the color that you're seeing in this plot is representing the intensity of the light. Dark red is a very intense color and a dark blue is a very low intensity. What's interesting is this walk was both indoor and outdoor. So you can tell when it's outdoor because you see that characteristic peak in the blue light that we saw earlier for sunrise. And then you can tell when I walk inside because of the fluorescent bulbs that were in the lab we were working out of. So you can see this characteristic shift in the light spectrum. At the bottom here we again have the x-axis showing time and then the y-axis is showing the left and right pupil diameters measured in millimeters. So you can see whenever I was inside the pupil diameter is pretty large. It's around 4 mm, but right when I go outside, it rapidly decreases to just above 2 to 3 mm and then periodically will spike up. And that's presumably for walking into shaded regions outside. And then right when I come inside, the pupils rapidly start to increase in size and they'll respond to even the subtle changes in light spectrum inside the lab we were working out of. Moving on to the machine learning. And then anything that deviates from this one 1:1 line is not a good estimation. All these different colors and dots you're seeing in the background here, they are the predictions of past models. For example, this past model might tell you the pupils are just above 2 mm. That would be the model's estimation. But the true pupil size could be anywhere between 2 mm all the way up to like 6 1/2 mm. There's a very large variation here. But if you're looking at the red and green circles and pluses here, that's the performance of the machine learning taking into account the full spectrum of visible light. We didn't just do this for average pupil diameter. We actually did this for the left pupil diameter, right pupil diameter, and then the average of the two. So we have the same kind of plot. It might be kind of small, but in the top left corner here, we have the estimated on the x-axis, the true on the y, and then anything sitting on this black line is a perfect estimation. The green circles here are showing the data from our training data set. So basically that's the data we used to develop the machine learning model and then these red pluses are data from a testing or validation data set. So that's data the model has never seen before. So you can see we can quantify how well the estimated and the true people size agree with each other through R squared score. The training data set had an R squared of 0.99 which is really good because the best R squ you can have is one which means a perfect fit. And then the validation is 0.96. So the machine learning basically nails the prediction of this very complicated interaction. And then it's the same thing for the right pupil diameter and then the average of the two. What's really nice about this machine learning approach that we used is not only do you get an empirical model of some complicated interaction, but you also get a predictor importance ranking. There was a major drawback in this preliminary work, namely that we had to drop a significant amount of data because the eyetracking glasses don't operate well in very bright ambient light conditions. Reason being is the eyetracking glasses actually use infrared light to track where the pupil is and ultimately estimate the pupil size. So when you go outside and there's the bright sunshine and a lot of infrared light outside, it'll actually wash out these infrared light sources that the glasses use to make the estimation. So basically any data record that was outside the sensing range of the eyetracking glasses we just dropped from the analysis. Some conclusions from this work is we use this holistic sensing and machine learning based experimental paradigm to derive highfidelity high accuracy empirical models of pupil diameter using spectral light information.
